art.title: foodmasku
art.medium: Performance, Photography, Video, NFT and Food
list: Began April 2020 during COVID lockdown, after a faulty Zoom pickle-face filter inspired the artist to create a kale salad mask.
"foodmasku" is a bilingual pun — "food + mask + u" in English; in Bahasa Indonesia/Javanese it reads as "food of my brother"
Named by The New York Times as one of five Instagram art accounts to follow (2020)
"Farm-to-Face" was commissioned for the UN SDG Action Campaign's UNITE TO ACT digital exhibition and unveiled at the 2023 UN SDG Action Awards, on the margins of the UN Food Systems Summit

The "Dining In" performance — two guests eating a meal off the artist's face — went viral multiple times across TikTok and Instagram, accumulating tens of millions of views and later featured on MTV's Ridiculousness
exhibitions: SXSW, NFT Gallery, Austin, TX — Livestream in Your Head (live performance with William Cobbing) — 2022
The Invisible Dog Art Center, Brooklyn, NY — Nafas (Dining In performance) — September 10 – October 15, 2022
Galerie Iham, Paris — What We Eat, Who We Are (solo show, Mangemasku series) — May 16, 2023
UN SDG Action Awards, UN Food Systems Summit — Farm-to-Face (UNITE TO ACT digital exhibition) — 2023
Essex Art Center, Lawrence, MA — Salon Show 2024 (group show, curated by Mariana Martins; featuring Good Luck Has Arrived, 2023) — July 13 – August 10, 2024
ArtVerse Gallery, Paris — All & Every (duo show with Tommy Hollenstein, presented during the Paris Olympics and Paralympics) — July 25 – September 8, 2024

press.title: Why this N.Y. artist is spending the pandemic turning his meals into face masks
The Instagram-Favorite Artist Who Makes Masks Out of Food Is the Star of a New Show About the Intersection of Art and Cuisine
Q&A: Foodmasku on channeling emotions into food mask art
From Farm to Face: How Foodmasku's Art Unites Creativity and Sustainable Food Systems
The internet thought allowing people to eat off your face was a job

art.description:
foodmasku is an ongoing performance, photography, and video series in which I make meals into masks and then eat them. It began in April 2020, during the early weeks of COVID lockdown, when a colleague's stuck pickle filter on a Zoom call prompted me to put kale on my face. It was the first I'd heard laughter in weeks. I felt it was important to find joy through art again. And so, a daily art practice was born.

Over the years the project has grown into a body of work at the intersection of trauma, food waste, and responsible consumption. The UN commissioned me to make a series on sustainable food systems, using the face as a site for meditating on how food reaches our tables, and what we choose to do with it when it arrives. Farm-to-Face was unveiled at the 2023 UN SDG Action Awards alongside the UN Food Systems Summit.

The series has also lived an unruly second life online. A performance staged at The Invisible Dog Art Center where two guests quietly eating dinner off my face was cut, recaptioned, and recirculated across TikTok and Instagram, racking up tens of millions of views and a segment on MTV's Ridiculousness. The runaway clip is frequently paired with fabricated captions about a "restaurant paying $10,000 a month for rich people to eat off your face," a viral rumor I continue to publicly debunk. The real piece is about labor, food inequality, and who gets to be seen at the table.

art.title: Post Papa
exhibitions: Essex Art Center, Lawrence, MA — Post Papa (on view) — through September 13, 2025
art.description:
Post Papa is a sketch series that I drew on Post-its while sitting beside my father in hospice.

The work was exhibited at Essex Art Center in Lawrence, MA through September 13, 2025, with half of all proceeds supporting the center, a non-profit arts community in the Merrimack Valley.

art.title: Since the Shooting
list: On July 5, 2013, I was shot by a stranger on a Brooklyn street while walking to the subway from my apartment in Bedford-Stuyvesant. The bullet was meant for someone else; it passed between two ribs, narrowly missed my heart, and lodged in my stomach.

Three strangers kept me alive until the ambulance arrived — a local barber who applied pressure to the wound, a woman from the neighborhood who kept me awake by yelling at me, and a man on his way to a job interview who fanned me with his résumé.
I spent four days in a sedated coma at Kings County Hospital, followed by seven months of physical therapy.

I was the first person Kathy Shorr photographed for her series SHOT 101 Survivors of Gun Violence in America, and have worked with Mayors Against Illegal Guns (now Everytown for Gun Safety), Moms Demand Action, Gays Against Guns, Teachers Unify to End Gun Violence, and many other organizations on gun-violence prevention.

press.title: Gunshot Victim Takes A Selfie A Day To Document His Healing
This is what gun violence looks like, for those who survive
Bed-Stuy Shooting Victim Meets the Man Who Saved His Life
101 Unflinching Portraits of Survivors of US Gun Violence

art.description:
Since the Shooting was an ongoing photographic practice of daily self-portraits featuring scars that run across my body from a bullet to the chest. It began the day I was released from Kings County Hospital in July 2013 and continued until the day of my shooter's trial.

On July 5, 2013 — the morning after the Fourth of July — a stranger came out of a passing car to shoot ata pregnant woman on my street in Bedford-Stuyvesant. The woman was shot in the leg, and the bullet that would have gone through her head hit me instead. The bullet went through me, between two ribs, narrowly missed my heart, and lodged itself in my stomach. I spent four days in a sedated coma and seven months in physical therapy. Three strangers kept me alive until the ambulance came: a barber who applied pressure to the wound, a neighbor who kept me awake by yelling at me, and a man on his way to a job interview who fanned me with his résumé.

The series began as a way to show my healing, one photograph a day, as a slow act of witnessing recovery from trauma. I carried the camera with me into hospital visits, onto planes, and into every apartment of every city I moved to. The scar became a reason to keep going. I continued the daily practice from the day I left the hospital through the day of my shooter's trial, and have continued making work in and around the series in the years since. I was also the first person photographed by Kathy Shorr for SHOT: 101 Survivors of Gun Violence in America.

art.title: If We Were XYZ
list: Sound and vocal composition by Jessika Kenney
EEG data, custom sleep-lab instrumentation, and sonification / visualization by Antonius Oki Wiriadjaja
Commissioned by Asia Society Museum for Creative Common Ground
exhibitions: Asia Society Museum, New York — If We Were XYZ (premiere, commissioned by Creative Common Ground) — October 18–19, 2019

press.title: A Performer Uses Brain-Mapping Technology to Scan Her Dreams
If We Were XYZ: Artistic Process

art.description:
If We Were XYZ is a three-hour durational performance by Indonesian artist Melati Suryodarmo, premiered at Asia Society Museum in October 2019 as part of the museum's interdisciplinary program Creative Common Ground. I served as the piece's artist-technologist: I built a self-devised sleep laboratory around Melati, recording EEG data while she slept, and then translated that raw neural data into the musical scores and visual systems that structured the performance. Vocalist and composer Jessika Kenney used the same dataset to write the piece's seven-part score — one movement for each dream.

The work sits at the intersection of Javanese mysticism, neuroscience, and Freudian psychology, asking what it means when the subconscious is made material. It was, in many ways, a question about intimacy: what do you owe a collaborator who has handed you the raw signal of their dreaming mind, and what kind of translation is faithful to that trust?

art.title: Other Work
art.description: Description of this work goes here.

tech.title: Computerized Embroidery
tech.medium: Brother Machines, Singer Machines, Embroidery Digitization
tech.description: Computerized embroidery sits at the intersection of craft and code. I work with Brother and Singer machines, converting vector artwork into stitch files through digitization software, controlling density, direction, and layering to get the texture and finish each piece demands. I run workshops on machine embroidery for institutions including New Inc, and I'm comfortable taking a design from Illustrator all the way through to a finished piece on fabric.

tech.title: 3D Printing
tech.description: I work with both FDM (fused deposition modeling) and SLA (resin) printing depending on what a project demands — FDM for structural parts and fast iteration, SLA for fine detail and smooth surfaces. I model for print in Fusion 360 and Onshape, dialing in tolerances, supports, and infill settings, and troubleshooting failed prints along the way. 3D printing is a core part of how I prototype and fabricate custom components for art and technology projects.

tech.title: Computer Numerical Control (CNC) Router
tech.description: I operate ShopBot CNC routers for cutting, carving, and milling wood and other sheet materials. My workflow runs from design in Fusion 360 through CAM toolpath generation and into the machine, where I manage feeds, speeds, bit selection, and fixturing. CNC routing lets me work at a scale and precision that's impossible by hand, and I use it for everything from structural fabrication to decorative surface work.

tech.title: Vinyl Cutter
tech.description: I use Cricut and professional vinyl cutters for signage, stickers, iron-on transfers, stencils, and custom decals. I prepare files in Adobe Illustrator, handling node cleanup and path optimization to ensure clean cuts. It's a fast, versatile tool I reach for whenever a project needs sharp graphics on a physical surface.

tech.title: Laser Cutter
tech.description: I work on Epilog laser cutters for cutting and engraving across wood, acrylic, leather, fabric, and paper. I prepare files in Illustrator, managing cut versus engrave layers, power and speed settings, and kerf compensation for tight-fitting assemblies. Laser cutting is one of my most-used fabrication tools: precise, repeatable, and fast, and I apply it to everything from product prototyping to art installations.

tech.title: Electronics Prototyping
tech.description: I'm comfortable moving from schematic to working circuit, breadboarding, soldering, and debugging electronics for interactive and wearable projects. I work with Arduino-based microcontrollers, select and wire sensors, design for power constraints, and solder everything from through-hole components to surface-mount repairs. Electronics prototyping is how my physical computing and e-textile projects come alive.

tech.title: Sewing and E-Textiles
tech.description: I design and build e-textiles, garments and soft objects that incorporate electronics, sensors, and conductive materials. I sew by hand and machine, construct soft circuits using conductive thread and fabric sensors, and integrate microcontrollers into wearable form factors. The course Talking Fabrics, which I built and taught at ITP and NYU Shanghai, grew directly out of this practice, exploring what happens when traditional craft meets programmable systems.

tech.title: Physical Computing
tech.description: Physical computing is the practice of building systems that sense and respond to the physical world, and it's been central to my work for over a decade. I design and build with Arduino, Raspberry Pi, and a range of sensors, connecting the digital and physical through hardware I wire, solder, and code myself. I taught physical computing at NYU Shanghai and work with students at all levels, from first breadboard to finished interactive installation.

tech.title: Creative Coding
tech.description: I write code as an artistic medium. Using JavaScript, p5.js, and Three.js, I build generative visuals, interactive browser-based experiences, and real-time graphics. I teach Creative Coding at Queens College CUNY, and my own practice uses code to explore pattern, motion, and emergent behavior. I'm comfortable with both structured programming and the looser, exploratory approach that generative work demands.

tech.title: Web Development
tech.description: I build websites and web applications from scratch in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, without reaching for frameworks when they're not needed. I understand layout, performance, responsive design, and accessibility, and I'm equally comfortable building a polished static site or a more complex data-driven interface. I teach Web Development at Queens College and BMCC, and this portfolio itself is hand-coded.

tech.title: Big Screens
list: Times Square and Rockefeller Center displays
IAC Building — world's largest screen at the time
Export for billboard and cinema-scale formats
tech.description: I produce work for some of the largest digital displays in the world, including Times Square, Rockefeller Center, and the IAC Building, which was the world's largest screen at the time. I know how to design for extreme aspect ratios and non-standard resolutions, manage synchronized multi-channel video output, and use openFrameworks to build custom playback and sync systems. Big screens demand a different way of thinking about image, motion, and scale.

tech.title: Workflow Automation
tech.description: I design no-code and low-code automation pipelines that connect the tools organizations actually use. At NYC FIRST, I built workflows linking Monday.com, Webflow, and Eventbrite, automating event publishing, syncing center open/closed status to a live website, and eliminating manual data entry across platforms. I work with n8n for custom logic and use automation to save teams hours of repetitive work every week.

tech.title: CAD
list: Designing for CNC, 3D print, and laser output
tech.description: I model in 3D using Autodesk Fusion 360 as my primary tool, designing parts for CNC routing, 3D printing, and laser cutting. I understand how to design for manufacturing, accounting for material tolerances, tool paths, and assembly. I also use Onshape in teaching contexts for its browser-based accessibility. CAD is how I move from an idea to something I can actually build.

tech.title: Adobe Suite
tech.description: I'm an Adobe Certified Professional in Graphic Design and Illustration using Adobe Illustrator, a tool I use daily for vector artwork, fabrication files, and print-ready design. I edit and produce video in Adobe Premiere and create motion graphics in After Effects. Across the full Creative Suite, I work with a level of fluency that comes from years of professional use and teaching.

tech.title: Figma & UI Design
tech.description: I design interfaces and prototypes in Figma, from early wireframes through to polished, interactive mockups. I think in components, understand design systems, and move fluidly between UX thinking and visual design. I also use Figma as a teaching tool, introducing students to interface design and the language of product thinking.

tech.title: Photography & Lighting
tech.description: I shoot with DSLR cameras and know how to set up and control studio lighting for portraits, product, and documentation work. I understand exposure, composition, and light as both technical and creative variables. Photography is part of how I document my own projects, from performance and fabrication work to events and student showcases.

tech.title: AI Tools & Workflows
tech.medium: Claude, ChatGPT, Flora, Generative Image Tools
tech.description: I work with AI tools not just as a user but as someone thinking critically about how they fit into creative and educational workflows. I use large language models, generative image and video tools, and AI-assisted coding daily, and I build workflows that put these tools to practical use. I'm also actively thinking about what it means to teach in an era when these tools are available to everyone.

tech.title: Video Editing & Production
list: Video production for art documentation and events
Export for large format and synchronized screen output
tech.description: I edit video in Adobe Premiere Pro and create motion graphics in After Effects, working across documentary, art, and event contexts. I understand storytelling through editing: pacing, sound, color, and structure, and I deliver across a wide range of output formats, from social media to large-format synchronized screen installations.

tech.title: Git & Version Control
list: GitHub for project hosting and collaboration
Managing code across projects and with students
tech.description: I use Git for version control across all my software projects, and GitHub for hosting, collaboration, and deployment. I'm comfortable with branching workflows, managing merge conflicts, using GitHub Pages for static site deployment, and bringing students into collaborative repositories. Version control is how I work cleanly and how I teach others to do the same.

tech.title: Teaching & Curriculum Design
list: Curriculum design for hands-on technical education
tech.description: I treat curriculum design as a creative practice, building courses around hands-on making with technical skills embedded in meaningful projects. I design and teach courses in creative coding, web development, e-textiles, physical computing, and interactive media at NYU, CUNY, and NYC FIRST STEM Centers. I believe the best technical education is grounded in curiosity, and I design learning experiences that make room for experimentation and productive failure.

tech.title: Max/MSP
list: Connecting physical sensors to sound and visuals
tech.description: Max/MSP is a visual programming environment for interactive audio and media, and I use it to connect physical inputs, sensors, MIDI controllers, and cameras to sound and visuals in real time. It's been part of my toolkit since studying at ITP, where real-time media processing was central to the program. I reach for it on live performance systems, installation work, and any project where audio and physical computing need to talk to each other.
